observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor lead the formation of a Disposal Committee to evaluate the current condition, verify the existence of PPEs donated or transferred to the barangays and schools, and expedite the disposal process for these items. This should occur concurrently with the One-Time Cleansing of accounts. To facilitate this process, we further recommend the following:
The Inventory Committee should include in the physical inventory assets that are still listed in the records but are not present at the municipal offices. This will help establish the existence and condition of these assets, as well as confirm the Municipality's ownership. A report should be created in accordance with the Annexes outlined in COA Circular No. 2020-006, as the disposal of these assets will take place simultaneously with the One-Time Cleansing of PPE accounts.

The Municipal Accountant should assist the Inventory Committee in identifying which assets have been transferred to various schools and barangays. Adjusting entries must be made once the required reports and the Annexes, including the Sanggunian Resolution on asset donations, are complete to facilitate the disposal of these assets.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to take the lead in enforcing sound control practices to ensure adherence to proper procurement procedures, including the accurate and timely recording of procured items. This includes clarifying the respective duties and responsibilities of the designated Storekeeper, the Inventory Committee, and other personnel involved in the inventory-taking process. In line with this, we further recommend the following:
The Designated Storekeeper to prepare and submit Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RCPI). This shall be submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively. Copies of this report shall also be submitted to the Municipal Accountant to provide for basis of recording for adjustments, if any, of inventory balances at the end of the year;
The Inspection Committee and the designated Storekeeper should ensure that the Acceptance and Inspection Report (AIR) is properly completed, with particular attention to accurately indicating the dates of delivery and inspection. This will help prevent the risk of paying for undelivered items or making duplicate payments. In line with this, we recommend the use of geotagged photos of deliveries, clearly reflecting the actual time, date, and location of the received items; and
The Municipal Accountant should ensure the accurate classification and recording of inventory accounts to guarantee the fair and reliable presentation of these accounts in the financial statements.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to exclude the unutilized Capital Outlay portion of the Mitigation Fund from transfers to the Special Trust Liability – DRRM account, in accordance with existing guidelines. Furthermore, we recommend the proper maintenance and regular updating of separate Registries of Appropriations, Allotments, and Obligations (RAAOs) for the Quick Response Fund (QRF), Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses (MOOE), and Capital Outlay (CO) components of the LDRRM Fund.

Lastly, appropriate adjusting entries must be made to correct the overstatement of the Special Trust Liability account and accurately reflect the corresponding amount in the government equity.

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to enjoin the following:
a) 	Direct the Municipal Budget Office to rigorously review the budgetary allocations for each office, ensuring compliance with legal and regulatory thresholds, particularly those governing EME or discretionary funds. These funds should not exceed two percent (2%) of the actual receipts from basic real property tax in the preceding calendar year and must be allocated exclusively to the Office of the Local Chief Executive, as prescribed by law; and
b) 	Instruct all offices to adhere to budgetary limitations, ensuring that actual disbursements do not exceed the approved appropriations.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to thoroughly evaluate the nature of expenses and ensure they are recorded under the correct expense accounts, in compliance with IPSAS 1, which mandates fair presentation. Additionally, the Municipal Budget Officer should strictly review the budgetary allocations for each expense account to prevent overdrafts and ensure that accurate amounts are proposed in the next budget review.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MTO and MAO to conduct monthly reconciliation of cash balances. To facilitate this, the MTO should update its cashbooks daily and compare their contents to the system-generated balances; promptly accomplish the Report on the Summary of Collection and Deposit, and submit the same to the MAO. Also, adjustments pertaining to cash balances should be recorded on the date of discovery in order to avoid erasures in the cashbooks.

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to enjoin the following:
For the Municipal Accountant to strictly review the completeness of attachments before releasing and signing the Accountant’s Advice for Local Checks; this is to compel the personnel concerned with prompt submission of supporting documents;
For the Municipal Treasurer not to process check payments to transactions with incomplete attachments, especially the Official Receipts, in order to ensure validity of the disbursements; and
For the Management to take immediate action to locate the 78 DVs and ensure their prompt submission. This includes identifying the personnel currently in possession of these documents and issuing a directive requiring their immediate compliance to prevent further delays and ensure proper documentation of transactions.

We have recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the BAC Chairperson and the Secretariat to furnish copies of contracts together with all the supporting documents to the COA within five (5) working days from execution thereof pursuant to COA Circular No. 87-278 and COA Memorandum No. 2005-027 as reiterated in COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009. This in effect would facilitate a more holistic review and evaluation of contracts.

We have recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to enjoin the TUPAD Authorized Coordinator of the Municipality, specifically the PESO Manager, to submit copies of DTRs, Daily Attendance Sheets, and TUPAD Completion Reports, as required under DO No. 239, s. 2023 of DOLE, for the beneficiaries.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person to undertake the following measures:
Conduct a thorough evaluation of the proposed or attributed PPAs to ensure they are genuinely responsive to identified gender-related issues and concerns;
Provide additional supporting documentation or justification to substantiate the GAD scores assigned to mainstreamed PPAs; and
Regularly update the GAD Database to facilitate the accurate identification, monitoring, and analysis of gender issues.

We have recommended and the management agreed to the following:
for the Municipal Mayor to direct the accountable officials to settle the disallowed amounts immediately. In the event that no payments are made, we further recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Treasurer, initiate the withholding of salaries and other emoluments of the concerned officials until the disallowances are fully settled.
For the Municipal Accountant to review and validate the unsettled balances attributable to each official to ensure that accurate amounts are reflected in the records.
for the Municipal Treasurer provide the Audit Team with certified true copies of the official receipts or Accountable Form No. 1 issued to officials as proof of their payments for the disallowed amounts. This will enable the Audit Team to issue the corresponding Notices of Settlement of Suspension, Disallowance, or Charge (NSSDC), thereby formally extinguishing the liability of the concerned officials.

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to enjoin the Municipal Accountant to include additional disclosures as to the total cash payments for acquisition of long-term assets/ PPEs. Moreover, it is essential to present the effects of transactions from the Notes to FS pertaining to PPEs to the Statement of Cash Flows and to Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts in a comparable and understandable manner.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant justified that the discrepancy was due to the recognition of cash payment basis in the cash flow, whereas in the SCBAA, gross amounts were recorded. On May 13, 2024, the Municipal Accountant submitted the revised Notes to FS providing for the necessary disclosures.
observations.reason: Despite the revision of the FS, discrepancies amounting to ₱1,523,691.92 between PPE under Note 7 and Note 20 was still noted.

observations.managementAction: The planning is still in progress.
observations.reason: The delay in conducting the actual physical inventory was due to team members’ existing obligations related to their primary job responsibilities. These obligations required them to prioritize the timely submission and completion of their regular reports and tasks. As a result, the team members were unable to dedicate adequate time and focus to the inventory counting process. Inventory counting was an additional responsibility outside of their core duties, and the lack of sufficient time to manage both their primary tasks and inventory count contributed to the delay. Consequently, this situation impacted their ability to complete the physical inventory in a timely manner.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to enjoin the Municipal Accountant to include additional disclosures as to the total cash payments for acquisition of long-term assets/ PPEs. Moreover, it is essential to present the effects of transactions from the Notes to FS pertaining to PPEs to the Statement of Cash Flows and to Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts in a comparable and understandable manner.

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal Assessor and the Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records pertaining to the Land account. Determine the acquisition cost of each land parcel composing the Land account. In cases where a parcel was acquired thru non-exchange transaction, such as donations, they shall determine the prevailing fair value at the date of acquisition.

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the Chairperson of Inventory Committee, together with the Municipal Assessor and Municipal Accountant, to exhaust all possible means to document the legality of ownership of the lots through a TCT in the name of the Municipality and submit a copy thereof to the Audit Team pursuant to Section 39 of PD No. 1445.

We have further recommended that funds be appropriated in the annual budget for the titling of the lands.

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the following:
For the Inventory Committee to complete the physical inventory count and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant, and Equipment (RPCPPE); and also require the submission of the report to the requiring offices within the prescribed period; and
For the Inventory Committee and Accounting office to reconcile the differences, if any, between the physical inventory report and the general ledger balance of each PPE account; subsequently, the Accountant shall identify if the difference represents PPE that are still existing, no longer existing but with pertinent records, or no longer existing and without pertinent records.

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the designated Supply/ Property Custodian thru the Inventory committee to complete the physical inventory count of inventories/ semi-expendable properties that are not yet distributed to end-users and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories (RCPI) to be submitted to the requiring offices; and to reconcile with the Accounting Office any difference between accounting records and physical count.

We have further recommended the Municipal Mayor to enjoin the Municipal Accountant to record the inventoriable purchases following the perpetual inventory method pursuant to the provisions of Section 114 of Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I and subsequently record the corresponding expense accounts only upon distribution.

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Treasurer to require the following:
A written explanation from the collecting officers for the noted delays in the remittance and the retention of collections within unreasonable period;
All collecting officers to remit their collections and accomplish their Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD) at close of each business day pursuant to Section 29 of the Manual of New Government Accounting System, Vol. 1 and see to it that no accumulation of cash from collections and mingling of funds shall be made by any collector to avoid loss, misapplication and/or unauthorized use; and
As for the deputized barangay collectors, to instruct them to remit their collections at least once a week or as soon as the collections reach P5,000.00, whichever comes first.

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Treasurer the following:
To ensure that all collections are deposited intact and daily, or on the next banking day pursuant to the provisions of Section 69 of PD No. 1445 and Section 32 of Manual on New Government Accounting System for LGUs, Volume I;
To instruct the Liquidating Officer to check the actual amounts of collections remitted by each collecting officer, and to regularly update the cashbooks on the amount of collections and deposits made for the day; and
should there be any cash overage incurred, demand written explanation from the Accountable Officer concerned. If no satisfactory explanation was provided, issue OR for the amount of overage in favor of the government. In case of cash shortage, demand immediately the payment of the amount of shortage.

We have recommended and Management agreed to instruct :
The Municipal Treasurer (MT) to send written confirmations to the payees holding the stale checks to ascertain whether or not these checks have been replaced. If replacements haven't been issued, demand the stale check for cancellation and proceed with issuing the replacement check. Following this, the MT should relay the results to the Municipal Accountant (MA);
Upon receiving the results of confirmation from the MT, the MA should effect immediately the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to present fairly the balance of the Cash in Bank – LCCA account in the financial statements.
Below is the suggested pro-forma adjusting entry:
For Check cancellation:
Cash in Bank-LCCA			xx
Accounts Payable				xx
For Replacement:
Accounts Payable			xx
Cash in Bank-LCCA			           xx

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to enjoin the Municipal officials to refrain from paying registration fee more than the allowable limit provided in the NBC No. 563 for seminars/ conference/ trainings conducted by NGOs or Private Institutions. Excess from allowable limit shall be at the expense of the participants.

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the BAC Secretariat to:
Ensure completeness of documentary requirements in each stage/ phase of procurement, particularly public bidding, to ascertain the eligibility of the contractors/ suppliers; provide the widest possible dissemination of advertisement hence assuring transparency of the procurement process; and assuring validity, propriety and legality of the disbursements pursuant to the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.
Explain why the Invitation to Bid as posted in the PhilGEPS was under “Pending” Status instead of “Active”.
We have further recommended that the Municipal Accountant to strictly review the completeness of the supporting documents before certifying and effecting payment for these transactions.

We have recommended and Management agreed on the following:
For all requisitioning officials to enumerate the specific type of items, without mentioning brand names, needed in the purchase and provide for estimated unit cost of each item; also, avoid including invalid items which are not subject to purchase in the PR such as honoraria and accommodations to make the costs comparable with those on RFQs and POs;
For the Municipal Treasurer to ensure compliance in the details of PR, particularly on the estimated unit costs and total costs, before affixing signature in the “Cash Availability” box in the PR; and
For the Municipal Accountant to strictly review the completeness of the details on PR, as compared with the RFQs and POs, before certifying and effecting payment for these transactions.

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the following:
For the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer, as well as all accountable officers concerned, to strictly comply with the timely preparation and submission of Disbursement Vouchers and all other documents salient to the transactions;
Also, for the Management to determine the location of the 127 unsubmitted DVs and/or the officials in possession of those documents, and demand immediate submission, otherwise Notice of Suspension may be issued for each of the unsubmitted DVs; and
For the Municipal Treasurer not to issue payment to transactions that are not supported by DVs and supporting documents, and those not bearing the required approval of the proper officials in order to ensure the validity and propriety of the transactions, to avoid disallowance in audit.

We have recommended and Management agreed that the GAD Focal Person prioritize the updating of the existing GAD Database which will aid in the identification of gender issues and full implementation of the PPAs in the GAD Plan and Budget; and to carefully evaluate the suggested and/or attributed PPAs to ensure that these effectively address gender-related issues and concerns.

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Local Finance Committee to inhibit the inclusion of projects, programs, and activities that do not promote socio-economic and environmental development and other expenditure items specifically not allowed under Item 3.2.5 of the JMC No. 1 of DBM, DOF and DILG as these do not directly support the mandate for which the fund should be appropriated and utilized.

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to enjoin the Training Management Team and the Local Finance Committee to observe prudence in spending government funds and strictly comply with the provisions of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2011-59 dated April 25, 2011 so as not to incur extravagant expenditures as cited in COA Circular No. 2012-003.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the concerned officials to immediately settle the disallowed amounts.

If no settlements were made by the concerned officials, we recommend that the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Treasurer, withhold the salaries of the concerned officials, as primary persons liable, until such settlement is made in compliance with the instructions provided in the COE issued.

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal Accountant to calculate the depreciation of Road Networks in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-008, excluding any salvage/residual value from the Road Network components.

a.	Restate the financial statement by reflecting the prior period adjustments amounting to ₱3,993,908.74 in the presentation of the CY 2022 comparative figures in compliance with IPSAS 3; 

b.	Provide additional disclosures in the Notes to the Financial Statements as to the information on the nature of the adjustments made in the beginning balance of the prior year financial statements, specifically in the SCNAE amounting to ₱2,019,382.18; and

observations.managementAction:
A memorandum has been issued to the inventory team instructing them to carry out the annual physical count of inventory. They are tasked to create plans and a timeline of activities that align in the Physical Inventory Plan (PIP). Additionally, they are directed to prepare a list of PPE items recorded in the PPELCs but not included in the Inventory Report, as well as PPE items included in the RPCPPE but not recorded in the PPELCs. Furthermore, after completing the physical count, for eh PPEs classified as non-existing /missing and for those without available records of accountability, they should conduct the procedures outlined under Items 7 and 8 of the same COA Circular for disposition and derecognition. 

Furthermore, management will convene a meeting to discuss the tasks to be undertaken following the adopting of the P50,000.00 threshold for recognizing PPE. A primary concern for the LGU is to ensure the security of semi-expendable items failing below the P50,000.00 threshold. Therefore, the individual assigned to record, update, and monitor these items must undergo form usage, and other related issues concerning this matter.
observations.reason: While a memorandum was issued by the Municipal Mayor to the Inventory Committee, the Committee has no action yet to accomplish the physical inventory count and implement the recommendations.

We have further recommended the Municipal Mayor to enjoin the Municipal Accountant to record the inventoriable purchases following the perpetual inventory method pursuant to the provisions of Section 114 of Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I and subsequently record the corresponding expense accounts only upon distribution.
observations.managementAction:
A memorandum has been issued to the designated Supply/Property Custodian by the Inventory Committee, assigning them the task of conducting a physical inventory count of inventories and semi-expendable properties that have not yet been distributed to end-users. Additionally, they are required to prepare the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories (RCPI) and submit it to relevant offices. Furthermore, the Supply/ Property Custodian has been instructed to reconcile any discrepancies between accounting and the physical count with the Accounting office. 

In addition, the management has also instructed the Municipal Accountant to record all purchases using perpetual inventory method, as outlined in Section 114 of the Manual on NGAs for LGU Volume I, and subsequently record the expense account upon distribution.
observations.reason: The delay in conducting the actual physical inventory occurred because team members have obligations with their primary job responsibilities, requiring them to submit and complete their reports on time. Inventory counting, being an additional designation.

observations.recommendation: The Treasurer’s office has been tasked to ensure that discrepancies or tampering with collection receipts are completely eradicated. To achieve this goal, management issued a memorandum to the Municipal Treasurer, instructing them to bolster internal controls in the preparation of official receipts. This involves ensuring consistency in details and amounts across the original copy and all duplicates. Moreover, it was recommended that the department centralize the collection process and implement a computerized collection system this year 2024 to prevent any potential tampering in the official receipts. Additionally, one of the management’s upcoming initiatives is to establish a one-stop shop in the market, equipped with computerized collection system, to minimize errors in the official receipt writing process. This will be implemented once the construction of the new market building is completed.
observations.managementAction: Municipal Mayor; Investigation team
observations.reason: The on-going construction of the market building is hindering the establishment of the planned computerized collection system to be installed in the market or one stop shop. Additionally, selecting and implementing a suitable computerized collection system that meets the municipality’s needs and budget, as well as training staff on how to effectively use the new system, are on-going priorities.

observations.managementAction:
The LCE has issued memorandum to the Treasurer’s Office directing compliance with specific protocols outlined in the Section 29 of the Manual on NGAs for LGUs. This directive mandates the deposit of all collections intact either on the same day or the following banking day. Additionally, collecting officers have been instructed to provide regular daily reports. Furthermore, the liquidating officer has been directed to thoroughly verify the actual amount of collections remitted by comparing the total amount collected in the Official Receipt to the actual amount remitted. 

Moreover, the Municipal Treasurer is mandated to request written explanations from collecting officers in cases of shortages or overages in the collection. The department is strictly instructed to ensure the integrity of government collections, emphasizing its importance. 

To support the Treasurer’s Office in meeting the requirement for daily deposits, management has prioritized the use of municipal vehicles for the MTO’s use.
observations.reason: Verification of the cashbook balances revealed that the recommendations were not yet fully implemented.

observations.managementAction:
The management has mandated the Gender and Development (GAD) focal person to ensure proper evaluation of what to include in the GAD Database. This includes verifying that the plans in the Programs, Projects, and Activities (PPAs) funded under the GAD Fund comply with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC Circular No. 2013-01 and 2016-01, as well as Section 36 of Republic Act (RA) No. 9710. 

To ensure compliance, the management enhance the Gender and Development Focal Point System (GFPS). This system will thoroughly evaluate and scrutinize each PPA to ensure alignment and effective addressing of identified gender-related issues and concerns. The team will assess proposed PPAs based in their potential impact o promoting gender equality, women empowerment, and inclusivity.
observations.reason: Review of the GAD Plan and Budget revealed several activities, such as tree planting, that are not responsive to any gender-related issues/ concerns.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Local Finance Committee to inhibit the inclusion of projects, programs, and activities that do not promote socio-economic and environmental development and other expenditure items specifically not allowed under Item 3.2.5 of the JMC No. 1 of DBM, DOF and DILG as these do not directly support the mandate for which the fund should be appropriated and utilized.
observations.managementAction: A memorandum has been issued to ensure that all personnel and departments adhere to the guidelines outlined in JMC No. 1 of DBM, DOF and DILG dated November 4, 2020. With regard to the expenditure detailed in AOM #2024-008 (2023), the disbursement of P15,7903.00 to Metro Clark Waste Management (MCWM) Corporation as a tipping fee for the disposal of residual waste aligns with the directives of the aforementioned memorandum circular. These expenditures are associated with Environmental Management Projects aimed at enhancing air and water quality standards. Furthermore, the total amount of P32,048.00 can be delineated as follows: P16,898.00 corresponds to the Solid Waste Tipping Fee, funded under 20% Development Fund, whereas P15,150.00 accounts for per diems incurred by personnel during official travel to Capas, Tarlac, was charged to the Office of the Mayor’s travelling expenses. All other projects listed in this AOM were deemed ion accordance with applicable guidelines.
observations.reason: The utilization of the 20% Development Fund is still on-going as of to-date. Also, no report pertaining to the recommendations was submitted.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to enjoin the Training Management Team and the Local Finance Committee to observe prudence in spending government funds and strictly comply with the provisions of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2011-59 dated April 25, 2011 so as not to incur extravagant expenditures as cited in COA Circular No. 2012-003.
observations.managementAction:
Municipal Mayor will issue a directive to observe prudence and comply with DILG Memorandum, Circular No. 2011-59 and COA Circular No. 2012-003. The directives include the following:

Conduct an internal review of current spending practices and identify areas for cost optimization.

 Formulate new guidelines emphasizing cost-saving measures and compliance with relevant circulars.

Organize training sessions for all the Training Management Team and Local Finance Committee on the new guidelines and prudent financial management. 

Implement a stricter approval process requiring detailed budget proposals and cost-effectiveness justifications.

Set up a monitoring system to track adherence to guidelines and regularly audit expenditures, report and evaluate.

Require post-event reports with cost analysis and gather participant feedback for continuous improvement.
observations.reason: The capacity building program is not yet conducted by the management as of date.

observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer will send demand letters to market stall occupants. Management will upgrade and revise the Revenue Code and market code, incorporating a due process in case of non-payment or overdue rental payments by the stall occupants. Sanctions will be specifically outlined to ensure due process for the agency to safeguard its rights. As the old market will soon be demolished, former occupants with delinquencies are prohibited from obtaining new stalls in the new market building. All delinquent payments must be settled before granting rights to have stalls in the public market of Asingan.
observations.reason: The implementation of the management action plan and audit recommendations has faced delays due to two factors. Firstly, the on-going demolition of the market building and construction of the new market has disrupted rental collection from stall occupants, complicating the enforcement of new policies. Secondly, the Market Code’s review by the municipal committee has slowed the development and approval of new regulations and sanctions. these issues have collectively impeded the timely execution of the planned actions and audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the concerned officials to immediately settle the disallowed amounts. 

If no settlements were made by the concerned officials, we recommend that the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Treasurer, withhold the salaries of the concerned officials, as primary persons liable, until such settlement is made in compliance with the instructions provided in the COE issued.
observations.managementAction:
The management has mandated the Human Resource Officer and the Municipal Treasurer to oversee the collection of the gasoline disallowance totaling to P1,414,206.70. to recover this amount, monthly salary deductions will be implemented for the concerned officers and officials. The HRO is responsible for coordinating the necessary payroll adjustments and ensuring the accurate implementation of these deductions. Meanwhile, the MT will manage the financial transactions related to the deductions, overseeing the collection and accounting of the recovered funds.

Initially, all affected officers and officials will be informed about the disallowance and the deduction plan, including a detailed breakdown of the total amount and the monthly deduction schedule. The HRO will then integrate the deduction plan into the payroll system, calculating and adjusting salaries accordingly. Consequently, the MT will track and manage the deducted amounts, ensuring they are deposited into the appropriate account designated for the disallowed funds.

Regular monitoring will be conducted to ensure accuracy and compliance with the deduction process. Periodic reports on the collection status will be provided to management. The process will be reviewed periodically to address any issues or discrepancies promptly, aiming to efficiently recover the disallowed funds and minimize delays in addressing the disallowance.
observations.reason: The delay in the implementation of the disallowance recovery is due to several key factors. Firstly, some of the responsible officers and officials have either died, resigned, or retired, making it difficult to recover the funds directly through salary deductions.  For those who are retired, there may be issues with financial capacity, as they might not be able to pay the disallowance in full. Additionally, active officers and employees are facing financial hardships, making it challenging for them to pay the full amount of the disallowance. They reply on their salaries to support themselves and their families, which further complicates the collection process.

observations.recommendation: 1.4	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting journal entries to correct the understatement of PPE accounts. Henceforth, ensure that costs of major repairs/rehabilitation and construction of infrastructure assets should be capitalized or recognized as property, plant and equipment in accordance with IPSAS No. 17.

2.14	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require:
The Municipal Accountant to strictly observe proper recognition of various assets, accounts payable and expenses by crediting this account only when the goods are delivered or services are rendered pursuant to NGAS for LGUs, Volume III, and to make the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the balance of the affected accounts;
The Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant a complete Report on Programs/Projects/Activities (PPAs) stating/and detailing the date of delivery/construction/completion; and
The Designated Supply Officer to provide the list of delivered items as a ready reference to facilitate the recording/recognition of assets and/or accrual of expenses at the end of each reporting period.

3.6	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive requires the Municipal Engineer to provide a quarterly report of ongoing PPAs to the Municipal Accountant to reconcile their respective records and make the necessary adjusting journal entries to reflect the correct balance of CIP account in the financial statements in compliance with the fair presentation under IPSAS No. 1.

6.6	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the MDRRMO to furnish a certified list of recipients as supporting documents for the disbursement vouchers for the relief operations. A summary or list of distributed goods and report on remaining inventory (if any) should also be prepared and submitted by the concerned officer in charge of the distribution to complete documentation of the transactions pursuant to Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445.

7.7	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Direct the MHO and the MENRO to coordinate with the DOH on the best course of action for the transport and disposal of COVID-19 wastes;
Instruct the MENRO to integrate COVID-19 Waste Management Plan into the LGU’s Solid Waste Management Plan; and
Advice the Local Development Council to review the programs under the Annual Investment Plan for the possible inclusion of programs related to COVID-19 Waste Management.

8.4	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) stop designating Job Order personnel as collecting officers since the Agency has no employer-employee relationship with them; (b) appoint and designate regular employees to discharge collecting functions in the market and (c) ensure strict adherence to existing regulations that all accountable officers for collections of revenues and receipts shall be properly bonded to safeguard government funds against loss and/or misuse.

9.6	We recommended that Management directs the Municipal Treasurer to maintain adequate internal control on cash by providing all concerned accountable officers a safe deposit box where unremitted or undeposited collections and accountable forms are stored for safekeeping to avoid the risk of loss pursuant to Section 101 of PD No. 1445.

11.6	We reiterate that the Local Chief Executive requires the:
Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal General Services Officer with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects.
General Services Officer in coordination with the Municipal Engineer to prepare Report on Local Road Networks and Local Road Network Property Card.
Municipal Accountant to prepare general ledger and the Local Road Network Ledger Card.
Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count of local road networks and prepare and submit the Report of Physical Count of the Road Networks System.
Concerned Offices to ensure coordination in the proper implementation of the accounting and reporting guidelines of COA Circular 2015-008 dated November 5, 2015.

12.6	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Creates an Inventory Team with Appraisal and Disposal Committee which will:
Determine the total unserviceable items together with their carrying amount and prepare the necessary documents to fast track the disposal of unserviceable PPE items;
Conduct an appraisal of the unserviceable properties and waste materials and furnish the Audit Team with a copy of the appraisal report thereof;
Facilitate the immediate disposal of the identified and verified unserviceable properties and waste materials to recoup any salvage value thereof.
Dispose the unserviceable properties based on the most appropriate mode of disposal pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular Nos. 89-296.
Enjoins the Supply Officer and the GSO to provide the necessary documents for the disposed unserviceable items which had a book value of ₱111,734.40.
Also, require both officers to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to determine the book value of the remaining unserviceable items.
Requires Municipal Accountant to derecognize the costs of the properties once the disposal thereof has been completed and submission of the necessary documents by the Supply Officer and the GSO.
Directs the Supply Officer and the GSO to provide first written communication to the Audit Team if a government property becomes unserviceable and strictly refrain from disposal or destruction of unserviceable items without the presence of the Audit Team or its authorized representative as required under Section 79 of the PD No. 1445.
Instructs the officials and employees concerned about the disposal and destruction of the unserviceable properties to submit a written explanation as to why the unserviceable PPE items were not presented to the Audit Team before their disposition.

13.5	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive through the LDRRM Officer submit the duly accomplish Acknowledgment Receipt for In-Kind Donations provided by COA Circular 2014-002 and the Municipal Vice Mayor through the Secretary of the Sangguniang Bayan to submit the authority from the Sangguniang Bayan to accept the donation as prescribed under Section 23 of RA 7160 to consider the donation valid and legitimate and to determine the proper accountability of the donated PPE.

14.4	We recommended that the General Services Officer (a) or other officer/s in charge of accepting deliveries of procured items, notify the Audit Team of the scheduled acceptance of all deliveries; and (b) submit copies of pertinent inspection reports within the prescribed period to facilitate timely conduct of inspection and other related audit procedures.

15.6	We recommended that the BAC through the BAC Secretariat ensure the submission of the perfected contracts and its supporting documents within five (5) working days from perfection as required under COA Circular No. 2009-001 for systematic and effective contract review to come up with timely and relevant audit results.

16.4	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Instruct Supply Officer to conduct pre-repair evaluation and post-repair evaluation/inspection of all government vehicles subject to repairs.
Require the Supply Officer to prepare a Certificate of Acceptance and secure warranty certificates and Waste Material Report to be approved by the LCE.

17.8	We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Engineer to undertake measures to comply with the pertinent provision of items 2 and 3 of COA Circular No. 2013-004 to enable inspection, validation and monitoring of PPAs by the Audit Team.

18.6	We recommended the Local Chief Executive through the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to appropriate and utilize the 20% Municipal Development Fund in conformity with the priority programs and projects set forth under DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 for the attainment of the desired socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes of the Municipal Government.

19.14	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Instruct the LDRRM Officer to maximize the utilization of the LDRRMF in accordance with the PPAs detailed in the LDRRMFIP.
Direct the LDRRM Officer to refrain from charging expenses that were not eligible for the calamity fund.
Enjoin Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to explain why similar documentary pictures attached to different disbursement vouchers were processed for payment, and
Require HRMO to organize and revisit the Municipality’s plantilla and sources of funds to be able to create a position that will assist the MDRRMO in the aspects of administration and training, research and planning, and operations and warnings.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive require the GAD Focal Person to establish and/or strengthen the monitoring and evaluation systems to assess the effectiveness and efficiency of their gender mainstreaming efforts, and the M&E activities shall be included in the annual GAD Plan and Budget.

22.6	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive through Municipal Treasurer and Market Supervisor:
Send demand letters when the stallholders become delinquent for at least three (3) months.
Institute legal actions against the delinquent stallholders who refuse to settle their past due accounts to improve the Municipality’s collection efficiency and to accumulate funds for the implementation of more beneficial programs and projects.
Instructs the officials and employees concerned on the market to submit a written explanation as to why market stalls had been assigned, subleased, or mortgaged by the original occupants were allowed for operation.

23.7 	We recommended that the HRMO and the Municipal Accountant ensure that payrolls of Job Order personnel and Consultants are supported with complete documentation pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001 and CSC Resolution No. 020790 dated June 5, 2002 prior to payment.

.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive create an
inventory team to complete the physical count of property, plant and equipment
and prepare and submit immediately the result of inventory conducted. Also,
require the facilitation of updating the accounting and property records, if any,
in order to come up with a reconciled PPE account balances as of December 31,
2017.

We have recommended that the OIC – Municipal Treasurer sent
monthly billing and demand letters when the stallholders become delinquent for
at least three (3) months. Institute legal action against the delinquent
stallholders who refuse to settle their past due accounts to improve the
Municipality’s collection efficiency and to accumulate funds for the
implementation of more beneficial programs and projects.

We have recommended the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to require its
collectors to effect their remittance daily, or when collections exceed ₱ 5,000.00,
whichever is applicable, and prepare it corresponding Reports of Collections
and Deposits.

We have recommended the Management to create an Inventory Team
and conduct periodic physical inventory of supplies and submit the Report of
Physical Count of Inventories not later than July 31 and January 31 of the each
year for first and second semester respectively in compliance with Section 124
of the Manual on New Government Accounting System for Local Government
Units, Volume 1.

We have recommended the Management to maintain proper inventory
accounts and its stock cards pursuant to Section 51 and 114 of the Manual on
New Government Accounting System for Local Government Units, Volume 1.

We have recommended that Management should:
a. Require the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to submit the Report of
Checks Issued and Report of Collections and Deposits together with
its paid disbursement vouchers, official receipts, and deposit slips
daily to the Accounting Office pursuant to Section 43 of the Manual
on NGAS and Sections 39 (1) and 100 of PD 1445;
b. Require the Municipal Accountant to record entries in the Journals
on a daily basis or as soon as the financial documents are submitted
by the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to prevent delay in the
preparation of financial statements; and
c. Require the Municipal Accountant to submit the disbursement
vouchers, official receipts, report of cash disbursements, and other
financial documents pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006dated
September 15, 2009.

We have recommended that the payment of salaries and wages of
employees of the Municipality be paid through ATM Payroll System to ensure
proper safeguard of fund and to avoid its possible misuse/application or loss.

We have recommended that Management prioritize the immediate
transfer of ownership of the acquired parcel of land amounting to ₱223,060.00
in the name of the Municipality of Asingan. Likewise, we recommend the
optimal coordination between the responsible municipal officials to ensure
proper and adequate legal documentation of the transfer of ownership.

Based on the noted deficiencies, we have recommended that
Management:
a. Continue the follow-up and monitoring of cash advances to ensure
full liquidation at year-end. Exhaust all possible means to recover the
unliquidated cash advance from Mr. Mangosong.
b. Stop the practice of granting cash advances to any official, officers
and employees without liquidating first his/her prior and outstanding
cash advance.
c. Strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002
dated February 10, 1997 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June
14, 2012 by requiring the timely liquidation of cash advances and the
attachments of a certification from the accountant that previous cash
advances have been liquidated and accounted for in the books. In
addition, stop processing payments of any cash advance in absence of
complete supporting documents.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant shall observe the
aforecited regulation in the recording of the income to avoid implication in the
preparations of the annual budget as its utilization.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the
Municipal Treasurer ensure that payrolls of Job Order personnel and
Consultants are supported with complete documentation pursuant to the
provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001 and CSC Resolution No. 020790
dated June 5, 2002 prior to payment.

We recommend the Municipality Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to
strictly and consistently comply with the posting requirement pertaining to
Notices Award in accordance with the provisions of law as well as guidelines
pertinent on the matter.

We have recommended the following:

Report of Cash Disbursement pursuant to the provision of Section 43
and 45 of the MNGAS. She shall also assign number to the Disbursement
Vouchers and to the Report of Collections and Deposit as compliance
with the instruction as provided on the Annex 24 and 41 of the Volume
II of the MNGAS. The numbering should be as follows:
000-00-00-000
Fund Code Year Month Series (one series per year)
2. We also recommend that the Municipal Accountant should refrain from
accepting disbursement vouchers from the Municipal Treasurer not
supported by the Report of Check Disbursement or by the Report of
Cash Disbursement as the case maybe. Moreover, she shall follow the
instruction in assigning number of JEV as provided by Annex 23 of the
Volume II of the MNGAS that there will be one series per year.
Likewise, the interest income shall not be recorded in the Cash Receipt
Journal instead General Journal shall be used in recording the same for
more accurate information.

We have recommended the Management to reconcile the RPCPPE with the property and accounting records. We have also recommended that the reports shall be dated to have basis in the determination on when the inventory was conducted. Likewise, property numbers or stock numbers, as well as the acquisition costs or appraise value of the properties acquired through donations shall be indicated in the report for proper identification and determination on whether the properties, equipments in the report were recognized in the books of accounts.
Furthermore, the report shall be categorized according to account classification to ascertain whether the actual physical count reconcile with the balance per subsidiary records. In addition, person accountable shall also be indicated in the report for proper determination of accountability in case of its misapplication.
Moreover, report for the unserviceable properties shall be prepared so that the same could be derecognized from the books or could be disposed in a prescribed manner.
Further, we have also recommended that inventory reports for land, land improvements, building and motor vehicles be submitted to this office to have a basis in the determination of its existence, condition, and whereabouts.

Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009- 001 dated February 12, 2009 laid
out the timelines for the submission of contracts and purchase orders and their
supporting documents, which states that:
“Within five (5) working days from the execution of a contract by the
government or any of its subdivisions, agencies or instrumentalities,
including government-owned and controlled corporations and their
subsidiaries, a copy of said contract and each of all the documents
forming part thereof, by reference or incorporation shall be furnished to
the Auditor of the agency concerned..."

In addition, Section 3.2.1 states that:
“A copy of any purchase order irrespective of amount, and each and every
supporting document shall, within five (5) working days from issuance
thereof, be submitted to the Auditor concerned..."

The late submission of the above-stated documents precluded the audit team
from prompt assessment to ascertain the propriety and completeness of the
transactions. Likewise, the existence of the deliveries, as well as its quality, quantity
and specifications could not be validated as scheduled due to delayed submission of
required documents.
